quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Simple pendulum period"?

options.text: A uniform spherical shell attracts external masses as if all its mass were at the center; inside the shell, gravitational force from the shell is zero
T = 2π√(L/g); depends only on length and gravitational acceleration for small-angle oscillations (θ < ~15°)
F_ext = dp/dt applied to rockets: thrust = v_exhaust × |dm/dt|, where mass decreases as fuel is expelled
Circular motion at constant speed; acceleration is purely centripetal (inward) with magnitude v²/r

quiz_questions.explanation: "Simple pendulum period" is defined as: T = 2π√(L/g); depends only on length and gravitational acceleration for small-angle oscillations (θ < ~15°). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Small-angle approximation"?

options.text: P = F·v = Fv cosθ; the power delivered at a specific instant when force F acts on an object moving at velocity v
An orbit with T = 24 hours at r ≈ 42,200 km from Earth's center; the satellite stays above one point on the equator
sinθ ≈ θ (in radians) for small angles; this linearizes the restoring torque and produces SHM
E_mech = KE + PE; the sum of kinetic and potential energies of a system

quiz_questions.explanation: "Small-angle approximation" is defined as: sinθ ≈ θ (in radians) for small angles; this linearizes the restoring torque and produces SHM. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Physical pendulum"?

options.text: p = mv; a vector quantity equal to mass times velocity, measured in kg·m/s
The gravitational force on an object is the vector sum of the gravitational forces from all other masses
The change in position vector Δx = x_final - x_initial; a signed quantity indicating direction, measured in meters
T = 2π√(I/(mgd)); a real extended body oscillating about a pivot, where I is the moment of inertia about the pivot

quiz_questions.explanation: "Physical pendulum" is defined as: T = 2π√(I/(mgd)); a real extended body oscillating about a pivot, where I is the moment of inertia about the pivot. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Restoring torque"?

options.text: τ = -mgd sinθ for a pendulum; for small θ, τ ≈ -mgdθ, giving angular SHM with ω = √(mgd/I)
Friction converts kinetic energy to thermal energy; work done by friction equals -f_k × d, always negative
A uniform spherical shell attracts external masses as if all its mass were at the center; inside the shell, gravitational force from the shell is zero
E_mech = KE + PE; the sum of kinetic and potential energies of a system

quiz_questions.explanation: "Restoring torque" is defined as: τ = -mgd sinθ for a pendulum; for small θ, τ ≈ -mgdθ, giving angular SHM with ω = √(mgd/I). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Energy in pendulum"?

options.text: f_k = μ_k N; the friction force during sliding, proportional to the normal force; typically μ_k < μ_s
ω = √(k/m) = 2πf = 2π/T; determines how fast the oscillation occurs
E = KE + PE = ½mv² + mgh; maximum KE at bottom, maximum PE at maximum angle
The vector sum of all forces acting on an object; determines the direction and magnitude of acceleration via F_net = ma

quiz_questions.explanation: "Energy in pendulum" is defined as: E = KE + PE = ½mv² + mgh; maximum KE at bottom, maximum PE at maximum angle. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "T = 2π√(L/g); depends only on length and gravitational acceleration for small-angle oscillations (θ < ~15°)"?

options.text: Average power
Internal vs. external forces
Simple pendulum period
Linear momentum

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Simple pendulum period".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "sinθ ≈ θ (in radians) for small angles; this linearizes the restoring torque and produces SHM"?

options.text: Power
Small-angle approximation
Angular momentum (rigid body)
Weight

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Small-angle approximation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "T = 2π√(I/(mgd)); a real extended body oscillating about a pivot, where I is the moment of inertia about the pivot"?

options.text: Elastic collision
Physical pendulum
Velocity and acceleration in SHM
Static equilibrium

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Physical pendulum".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "τ = -mgd sinθ for a pendulum; for small θ, τ ≈ -mgdθ, giving angular SHM with ω = √(mgd/I)"?

options.text: Total orbital energy
Restoring torque
Parallel axis theorem
Torque-angular momentum relation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Restoring torque".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "E = KE + PE = ½mv² + mgh; maximum KE at bottom, maximum PE at maximum angle"?

options.text: Work-Energy Theorem
Horsepower
Torque-angular momentum relation
Energy in pendulum

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Energy in pendulum".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Pendulums is accurate?

options.text: Geosynchronous orbits, escape velocity (v_esc = √(2GM/r)), and energy in orbits (E = -GMm/2r) are key applications of gravitational and energy concepts.
Vector addition, subtraction, dot product (A·B = ABcosθ), and cross product (A×B, magnitude = ABsinθ) are essential operations throughout mechanics.
Pendulums are oscillating systems where gravity provides the restoring torque.
Work and Energy provide a scalar framework for analyzing motion without tracking forces and accelerations over time.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Pendulums as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Pendulums?

options.text: Springs and Wave Motion connect oscillating springs to the fundamental properties of waves.
The simple pendulum (point mass on a massless string) has period T = 2π√(L/g) for small angles, independent of mass and amplitude.
For variable forces (such as springs: F = -kx), integration is essential: W_spring = ∫(-kx)dx = -½kx².
The angular frequency ω = √(k/m) depends on system parameters (spring constant and mass), not amplitude.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Pendulums as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Pendulums?

options.text: Newton's Second Law for rotation in momentum form gives τ_net = dL/dt.
The critical condition at the top of a vertical loop (minimum speed for maintaining contact) occurs when the normal force equals zero: mg = mv²/r, giving v_min = √(gr).

Torque and Rotational Dynamics extend Newton's Second Law to rotation: τ_net = Iα, where torque τ = r × F (magnitude rF sinθ) is the rotational analog of force, and I (moment of inertia) is the rotational analog of mass.
The physical (compound) pendulum accounts for distributed mass: T = 2π√(I/(mgd)), where I is the moment of inertia about the pivot and d is the distance from pivot to center of mass.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Pendulums as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Pendulums?

options.text: Both approximate SHM for small oscillations.
Collisions are classified by whether kinetic energy is conserved.
Vectors and Components provide the mathematical language for describing physical quantities with both magnitude and direction.
This inverse-square law governs planetary orbits, satellite motion, and tidal effects.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Pendulums as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Pendulums?

options.text: Work is defined as W = ∫F·dr (the dot product integral of force along the displacement path), and the Work-Energy Theorem states W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mv₀².
Momentum and Impulse are deeply connected: the impulse (J = ∫F dt) delivered to an object equals its change in momentum (Δp = mΔv).
The center of mass moves as if all the system's mass were concentrated there with F_ext = Ma_cm.
Pendulums are oscillating systems where gravity provides the restoring torque.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Pendulums as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Pendulums?

options.text: Amusement park ride engineering ensuring loop-the-loop roller coasters maintain sufficient speed at the top for the normal force to remain positive
Designing pulley systems (block and tackle) to provide mechanical advantage for lifting heavy loads in construction
Foucault pendulum demonstration proving Earth's rotation by observing the slow precession of a long pendulum's swing plane over hours
Baseball physics analyzing the impulse of a bat on a ball from high-speed camera force-time data to optimize hitting mechanics

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Pendulums.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Pendulums?

options.text: Centrifuge design in molecular biology calculating the centripetal acceleration needed to separate cellular components by density
Analyzing car crash data using kinematic equations to calculate braking distances and deceleration rates for traffic safety engineering
Grandfather clock design using the precise period T = 2π√(L/g) of a pendulum to regulate timekeeping mechanisms
Figure skating spins: pulling arms in reduces I, increasing ω to conserve angular momentum (demonstrating I's physical significance)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Pendulums.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Pendulums applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Calculating the angular deceleration of a flywheel when braking force is applied to determine stopping time and number of revolutions
Seismometer design using physical pendulums with long periods to detect and record slow ground motion during earthquakes
Engineering spring-loaded mechanisms (car bumpers, mattresses) by calculating work stored and released using W = ½kx²
Communication satellite placement in geosynchronous orbit at r ≈ 42,200 km ensuring 24-hour period for continuous coverage of a fixed ground region

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Pendulums.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Pendulums?

options.text: Automobile engine specifications using power curves (horsepower vs. RPM) to match engine performance to vehicle weight and desired acceleration
Foucault pendulum demonstration proving Earth's rotation by observing the slow precession of a long pendulum's swing plane over hours
Flywheel energy storage systems using high-I, high-ω wheels to store kinetic energy for regenerative braking in vehicles and power grid stabilization
Space elevator feasibility studies computing the potential energy change of lifting payload from Earth's surface to geosynchronous altitude versus traditional rocket propulsion

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Pendulums.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Pendulums in practice?

options.text: Nuclear physics analyzing particle collision products using conservation of momentum in two dimensions to identify unknown particles
Firearms forensics using conservation of momentum to calculate bullet speed from the recoil velocity of a ballistic pendulum
Analyzing inclined conveyor belt forces in mining operations to determine motor power needed to transport ore uphill
Grandfather clock design using the precise period T = 2π√(L/g) of a pendulum to regulate timekeeping mechanisms

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Pendulums.
